info.title: Arazzo Workflow Control Plane API
info.summary: Inspect and remediate durable Arazzo workflow runs.
info.description:
A REST surface over the Arazzo durability control plane (`ISecuredWorkflowManagement`, plan §11): list and inspect runs, resume a faulted run, cancel a run, and reap terminal ones. It is the workflow-level analogue of dead-letter inspection and redelivery. Every control action takes a single-owner lease and writes under optimistic concurrency, so concurrent operators — or an operator and a worker — cannot conflict. This document is the contract a CLI (`arazzo-runs`) and other clients are generated against; an implementation maps each operation onto the corresponding `ISecuredWorkflowManagement` method over a chosen durability store.

## Authorization responses

Every operation declares a capability-scope `security` requirement (design §14.1) that the deployment's authentication layer enforces uniformly. So in addition to the responses listed on each operation, any secured operation may return **401 Unauthorized** — no credentials were supplied, or the access token is missing, invalid, or expired (the `WWW-Authenticate` header names the scheme) — or **403 Forbidden** — the caller is authenticated but the token lacks the required capability scope. These ambient authentication/authorization responses are uniform across the surface and are not repeated on each operation. Operation-specific **403**s — row-level access (the caller may see a row but is not permitted to act on it, §14.2) — are documented individually where they apply. A deployment that fronts this API with a cookie-session back-end-for-front-end (BFF) additionally requires an anti-forgery header (`X-CSRF`) on cookie-authenticated, state-changing requests; see control-plane design §16.3.

servers.description: The control-plane host (a service the operator deploys alongside its durability store).

tags.name: runs
tags.description: Visibility and remediation for durable workflow runs.

tags.name: catalog
tags.description: Publish and read versioned, runnable workflow definitions: add catalog versions, edit their governance metadata, read packages/workflows/executors/sources, validate inputs, and start runs (capability scopes catalog:read / catalog:write / catalog:purge, plus runs:write to start a run).

tags.name: runners
tags.description: Read the set of registered runners that host and execute catalog workflow versions (capability scope runs:read).

tags.name: security
tags.description: Author the row-security policy: rules and claim→rule bindings (capability scopes security:read / security:write).

tags.name: credentials
tags.description: Manage source credential bindings (design §13): operator-managed references to secret material in an external secret store, plus non-sensitive auth metadata. The control plane stores and returns references only — it never reads, returns, or resolves secret material (capability scopes credentials:read / credentials:write).

tags.name: administrators
tags.description: Manage who may publish and administer a base workflow id (design §15): the set of deployment-stamped administrator identities. Identities are named via the deployment-mapped grant {dimension,value} vocabulary, never raw internal tags; mutations are authorized by current-administrator membership (capability scopes administrators:read / administrators:write).

tags.name: environments
tags.description: Manage governed, reach-scoped deployment environments (design §7.7): create/list/read/update/delete environments and govern each one's administrators (who may manage it and approve promotions into it). Creating an environment grants the creator administration; environments are governed exactly like a workflow (capability scopes environments:read / environments:write, with current-administrator membership applying to governance mutations).

tags.name: sources
tags.description: Manage first-class, reach-scoped source registrations (design §7.6): register/list/read/update/delete the OpenAPI/AsyncAPI documents a workflow references by name in its sourceDescriptions. A source is registered once and resolved immutably by the catalog; its per-environment credentials are managed separately (the credentials surface). Reach-scoped management — there is no administrator set; reach membership is the gate (capability scopes sources:read / sources:write). The registered document is returned only on read of a single source, never in the list.

tags.name: workspace
tags.description: Designer working copies (workflow-designer design §4.1): mutable Arazzo documents saved during development without minting catalog versions. Reach-scoped, etag-guarded; publish mints an immutable catalog version.

tags.name: debugRuns
tags.description: DEBUG a working copy (workflow-designer design §18): start, step/resume, inspect, inject a message into, cancel, and purge a durable debug run in a development-class environment. These are debugging affordances — a driven, forward-only run under governed dev credentials with no production analogue; message injection stands in for the real publisher (capability scopes workspace:write + runs:write; read is workspace:read).

tags.name: github
tags.description: Brokered GitHub integration (workflow-designer design §4.7): the control plane holds an OAuth App's credentials and each principal's user token — the kit never sees a GitHub credential. Authorship is the signed-in user's GitHub-held git identity, stamped by GitHub rather than composed by the control plane; reach is the OAuth model's — whatever the signed-in user can see (the repo scope), so a source document anywhere the user can read is reachable; token custody is server-side, keyed by control-plane principal; there are no unattended actions. Deployment-configured (a deployment that brokers no OAuth App refuses these operations).

tags.name: availability
tags.description: Make a workflow version available in a deployment environment — "promotion" (design §7.8). Availability is an additive, many-to-many matrix (workflow version × environment): a version may be available in many environments and an environment may have many versions of one workflow available at once; making one version available never retires another. Making a version available (or withdrawing it) is governed by the TARGET environment's administrators, and is allowed only where the version is ready in that environment — every source the version references has a usable credential there (design §7.7), and, where the environment requires evidence (workflow-designer design §4.6), the version's server-attested scenario suite is green. Reading availability is reach-scoped to the workflow / environment (capability scopes availability:read / availability:write).

tags.name: accessRequests
tags.description: Request and approve elevated, time-bound access to a workflow (design §16.5). Any authenticated principal may submit a request and withdraw their own; an administrator of the target workflow (§15) approves, denies, or revokes. An approval writes a single capped, time-boxed grant (run access only, scoped to the workflow); an eligible requester self-elevates without a human approver. These operations are authorized by the per-workflow administrator gate, not a global capability scope.

tags.name: availabilityRequests
tags.description: Request and approve making a workflow version available in an environment — "promotion" requests (design §7.8). When a principal cannot make a version available directly (they do not administer the target environment), they raise a request; an administrator of that environment approves or denies it, and the requester may withdraw their own. The subject is (workflow version → environment); approval makes the version available (subject to the same §7.7 readiness gate as a direct make). These operations are authorized by the per-environment administrator gate, mirroring the §16.5 access-request inbox, not a global capability scope.

tags.name: runnerAuthorizations
tags.description: Authorize which runners may serve a deployment environment (design §5.5). Receiving an environment's runs means receiving its credentials, so a runner may not self-assert into an environment: it enters Pending on registration and is dispatchable only once an administrator of that environment authorizes it. An administrator can quarantine a faulted runner to exclude it from new dispatch while its in-flight runs drain (reinstating it later without re-registration), or revoke a compromised runner to remove it permanently and fence its in-flight work. Listing an environment's runners and deciding them are governed by that environment's administrators (scopes environments:read / environments:write); the cross-environment approver inbox spans every environment the caller administers, mirroring the §15.4 availability-request inbox.

tags.name: identity
tags.description: Resolve real grantees (person/team/role/workflow) to their exact deployment-stamped identity for the view/operate/administer grant pickers (design §16.5.4), and report the caller's own identity. Read-only: resolution uses a pluggable directory and a store-indexed typeahead of identities the control plane has already observed, so the picker never hand-assembles a {dimension,value} tuple that could silently match no one.

get.description: The bounded summary for a single resolved grantee: its identity, the capability scopes its bindings confer, and the environments it administers. The unbounded lists — reach bindings, administered workflows, and credential usage — are the keyset-paged sub-resources GET /access/grants/{reach,administered,credentials}. The grantee is passed as an opaque URL-safe token: the base64url encoding of a resolved grantee's JSON (a ResolvedGrantee object — kind, value, identity, source, complete — exactly as returned by GET /identity/grantees), round-tripped verbatim by the client. Reach-scoped. Selection is based on the grantee's resolved sys: identity, so a live token may carry additional non-identity claims (e.g. groups) not represented here, and wildcard ('*') bindings that apply to every principal are omitted.

A keyset page of the security bindings whose claim matches the grantee's identity, each with its per-verb read/write/purge reach — the grantee's reach, one page at a time. Delete a binding to revoke that access. Reach-scoped; wildcard ('*') bindings are omitted. See GET /access/grants for the grantee token and the bounded summary.

200.description: One page of the grantee's reach bindings.

get.description: A keyset page of the base workflows the grantee administers (membership: an administrator identity contained in the grantee's identity), each enriched with its representative catalog version so the row reads without a detail fetch. Reach-scoped. See GET /access/grants for the grantee token and the bounded summary.

200.description: One page of the workflows the grantee administers.

get.summary: The credentials a grantee's runs may use (paged)
get.description: A keyset page of the source-credential bindings the grantee's runs may use — the credentials whose usage grant is scoped to the grantee's identity (a label-superset over the binding's usage grant; shared deployment-wide bindings are omitted, §6.1). Reach-scoped. See GET /access/grants for the grantee token and the bounded summary.

200.description: One page of the credentials the grantee's runs may use.

get.description: Returns a page of runs matching the visibility query. Backed by the store's wait/visibility index (the same index Tier 2 uses for wakeups), so the filters are answered server-side.

PURGE.description: Reaps completed and cancelled runs last updated strictly before `olderThan`, up to `limit`. Non-terminal runs (including faulted ones, which are terminal-but-recoverable) are never purged.

get.description: Returns a run's management detail — status, cursor, wait/fault detail and etag — from its authoritative checkpoint.

delete.description: Permanently deletes a single run by id, regardless of status. Unlike `PURGE /runs` (which reaps only old terminal runs in bulk), this removes exactly the named run — the operator's explicit "remove this" action. Destructive: requires the `runs:purge` scope.

get.summary: Get a run's recorded step outputs
get.description: The step journal the run's authoritative checkpoint attests: each step the run executed, in execution order, with its outcome, attempt, time window, and the outputs value it recorded (ADR 0050). A run whose checkpoint predates the journal reports only the steps that recorded outputs, with no status or timing invented. Reach-gated exactly like the run detail (outside read reach reads as not found). Because step outputs can carry the sensitive data the workflow processed (for a KYC flow, identity data), reading them is a distinct disclosure tier ABOVE run-metadata visibility: this operation demands the `runs:outputs:read` capability scope IN ADDITION to `runs:read`, so a reach-scoped observer who may see a run cannot read its payloads without it. Outputs a version classifies sensitive, and fields marked sensitive in a workflow's output schema, are redacted from the journal unless the caller additionally holds write reach on the run (§14.2).

200.description: The recorded step outputs, in recording order (sensitive content redacted unless the caller holds the stronger grant).

post.description: Resumes a faulted run, re-executing it from its last checkpoint (the faulted step). Only a run in the `Faulted` state can be resumed. Returns the run's new detail on success (200); a run that does not exist or is out of read reach is 404, a readable run outside write reach is 403, and a run that is not in the `Faulted` state conflicts (409).

200.description: The run was resumed; the body is its new detail.

post.description: Cancels a non-terminal run, marking it `Cancelled` (any fault record is kept for post-mortem visibility). Returns the run's new detail on success.

200.description: The run was cancelled; the body is its new detail.

get.description: Returns a keyset page of the workflow runners currently registered with the control plane (ordered by runnerId): each runner's identity, liveness timestamps, capacity, supported transports, and the catalog versions it hosts. Runners self-register and heartbeat directly against the durability layer; this endpoint reads that registry for observability. Bounded by limit and resumable via the page token.

Counts the registered runners the caller's read reach admits (§5.5/§14.2), bounded by the server's cap — no runner rows are returned (for the list footer). Reach here is a per-row predicate over each runner's reach tags, so the count matches the reach-filtered listRunners exactly. When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

200.description: The bounded count of visible runners.

post.description: Uploads a new immutable workflow document package (the Arazzo workflow plus its referenced OpenAPI/AsyncAPI sources) as `multipart/form-data` — a `package` file part (shaped as the `CatalogPackage` schema) plus `owner` and `tags` parts. The store assigns the next version number, rewrites the workflow id to the versioned form (`{baseWorkflowId}-v{n}`), and records the content hash. The submitted workflow id must not already carry a `-vN` suffix.

package.description: The workflow document package envelope (its shape is the `CatalogPackage` schema), uploaded as a file.

tags.description: Free-form tags for display and filtering.

securityTags.description: Non-internal security tags (§14.2 reach labels) to stamp on the version at add, alongside the deployment's internal tenant tag. The reserved internal-tag prefix is rejected (400).

201.description: The version was added; the body is its metadata (the assigned version, versioned id and hash). The documents are fetched via the links.

get.description: Returns a page of catalog version summaries matching the query — free-text over title/description, base workflow id, tags (AND-matched), status, and owner. Keyset paginated.

200.description: A page of matching catalog versions.

PURGE.description: Bulk-deletes obsolete catalog versions that have no referencing workflow runs.

get.summary: List the versions of a workflow

200.description: The versions stored under this base workflow id.

get.description: Returns the version's metadata — content hash, governance metadata, audit fields and the list of documents in the package — plus, for versions published with scenario evidence, the publish-evidence summary (the badge's data; the full per-scenario record is the evidence endpoint's). The package and its individual documents are fetched via the links.

patch.description: Updates the mutable governance metadata (owner, tags, status). The immutable package, hash, title and description are unchanged. Records `lastUpdatedBy`; setting `status` to `Obsolete` also records `obsoletedBy`.

delete.description: Permanently deletes a single version. Refused with `409` while any workflow run references its versioned workflow id.

get.description: Streams the version's immutable, canonical document package envelope (the Arazzo workflow plus its referenced sources, shaped as the `CatalogPackage` schema) as a single binary download — the self-contained, hash-verifiable artifact that can be handed to the code-generation service.

post.description: Runs the deterministic simulator over the version's IMMUTABLE stored package — the workflow document and its embedded sources, exactly as published — with the same request shape as simulateWorkingCopy (scenario, stop condition, budget) and the same complete trace back (workflow-designer design §4.3/§8). Re-verify evidence, or explore a regression, without a working copy. Mutates nothing.

200.description: The structured trace, complete up to the stop condition.

422.description: The version's document does not compile to an executable workflow (e.g. it references another Arazzo document as a source).

get.description: The server-attested evidence recorded at publish (workflow-designer design §4.6): engine version, package hash, the scenario suite's verdicts. 404 when the version predates evidence or was published without scenarios.

get.summary: Get a version's Arazzo workflow document
get.description: Returns just the Arazzo workflow document for this version (with the versioned workflow id), without the OpenAPI/AsyncAPI sources. Convenient for the UI when only the workflow definition is needed.

get.summary: Get a version's precomputed schema metadata
get.description: Returns the precomputed schema-metadata document baked into the package — the typed shape of each workflow's inputs and each step's resolved outputs (and, progressively, requests and responses) — so UIs can render strongly-typed forms (a typed patch/output builder, a workflow editor) without re-parsing the OpenAPI/AsyncAPI sources. 404 if the version carries no baked metadata.

get.summary: Download a version's compiled workflow executor assembly
get.description: Streams the compiled workflow executor assembly (a .NET DLL) baked into the package as a single binary download — the artifact an execution host dynamically loads to run the workflow. 404 if the version is not runnable (carries no baked executor).

Returns the executor manifest baked alongside the compiled assembly — the target framework, the assembly digest binding the DLL to this version, the entry type, and the declared sources — so a runner can verify and load the executor. 404 if the version is not runnable (carries no baked executor).

post.summary: Validate a value against a schema in a version
post.description: Validates a JSON value against the true JSON Schema of a target within this version's package — a workflow's `inputs`, a step's request or response body, or a step's `outputs` object — using a full JSON Schema validator (not the lossy precomputed metadata). Returns a structured result (valid plus any errors with their instance locations); a malformed value still yields `200` with `valid: false`. Returns `404` when the version or the requested target schema cannot be resolved.

post.summary: Start a run of a workflow version
post.description: Triggers a new run of this runnable version in a deployment environment (design §5.5): validates the supplied inputs against the version's baked inputs schema, pins the run to the required `environment`, then creates a Pending run that a runner serving that environment claims and executes asynchronously and durably. The run is pinned to the environment at start — it selects the credential set and constrains dispatch to runners authorized to serve it. Supply an `Idempotency-Key` header to make the start idempotent: a repeated request carrying the same key returns the run the first created rather than starting a duplicate. Returns 202 with the run id; observe the run via the runs endpoints. 404 if the version does not exist, or the environment does not exist or is outside the caller's reach; 409 if the version is not available in the environment (§7.8), is not runnable (carries no executor), or no registered runner currently serves the environment; 422 if the inputs fail validation.

parameters.name: environment
parameters.description: The deployment environment to pin the run to (design §5.5). The version must be available in this environment (§7.8) and the environment must be within the caller's reach; the run uses this environment's credential set and is dispatched only to runners serving it.

202.description: The run was accepted and is pending execution on a hosting runner.

422.description: The inputs failed validation against the version's baked inputs schema.

get.summary: Get a single source document from a version
get.description: Returns one named source document (an OpenAPI or AsyncAPI document) referenced by this version's workflow. The available source names are listed in the version metadata's `sources` array.

get.summary: List the environments a workflow version is available in
get.description: Returns a page of availability entries for this version — the deployment environments it has been made available in (design §7.8), ordered by environment. Visible to a caller who can read the workflow version.

get.summary: Count the environments a workflow version is available in
get.description: Counts the environments this version has been made available in (design §7.8), bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for the list footer). Visible to a caller who can read the workflow version (404 otherwise). When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

200.description: The bounded count of environments the version is available in.

put.summary: Make a workflow version available in an environment
put.description: Makes this workflow version available in the environment (design §7.8) — additive, never retiring another version. The caller must be a current administrator of the TARGET environment (403 otherwise), and the version must be ready in that environment: every source it references must resolve a usable credential there (design §7.7), else 409 with the missing sources; and where the environment requires evidence (workflow-designer design §4.6), the version's server-attested scenario suite must be green, else 409 (evidence-required). Idempotent — making an already-available version available again returns the existing entry (200).

200.description: The version was already available (the existing entry).

delete.summary: Withdraw a workflow version's availability in an environment
delete.description: Removes this version's availability in the environment (design §7.8). The caller must be a current administrator of the target environment (403 otherwise). Does not affect any other version or environment.

get.summary: List the configured ordered tag dimensions
get.description: Returns the deployment's ordered tag dimensions (e.g. classification) and their labels in ascending order, so an authoring UI can offer ordered (<, <=, >, >=) rule templates with the exact labels the policy enforces.

Counts the rules matching q, bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for the list footer). Same q filter as searchSecurityRules (a case-insensitive substring of the name or expression). When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

parameters.name: q
parameters.description: Free-text filter matched against each rule's name and expression (case-insensitive contains).

200.description: The bounded count of matching rules.

get.description: Returns a keyset page of rules ordered by name, bounded by limit and resumable via the page token; q filters by a case-insensitive substring of the name or expression.

parameters.name: q
parameters.description: Free-text filter matched against each rule's name and expression (case-insensitive contains).

post.description: Creates a named rule; the expression is validated against the security-rule grammar.

put.description: Replaces the rule's content; the expression is validated against the security-rule grammar.

get.description: Counts the bindings matching q, bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for the list footer). Same q filter as searchSecurityBindings (a case-insensitive substring of the claim type, claim value, or description). When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

parameters.name: q
parameters.description: Free-text filter matched against each binding's claim type, claim value, and description (case-insensitive contains).

200.description: The bounded count of matching bindings.

get.description: Returns a keyset page of claim→rule bindings ordered by (order, id), bounded by limit and resumable via the page token; q filters by a case-insensitive substring of the claim type, claim value, or description.

parameters.name: q
parameters.description: Free-text filter matched against each binding's claim type, claim value, and description (case-insensitive contains).

post.description: Creates a claim→rule binding; the store assigns its id.

get.description: Returns a page of source credential bindings (references and non-secret metadata only), ordered by sourceName then environment. Page with `limit` and the opaque `pageToken` from a previous page's `nextPageToken`.

post.description: Creates a binding for (sourceName, environment). The body carries references (a SecretRef of the form scheme://locator[#version]) and non-secret metadata only; a value that is not a well-formed SecretRef is rejected (400). Conflicts if a binding for that (sourceName, environment) already exists.

put.description: Replaces the references and non-secret metadata of an existing binding; the (sourceName, environment) identity and the created-* audit fields are immutable. Rotation is by changing the reference. A value that is not a well-formed SecretRef is rejected (400).

get.description: Lists the working copies in the caller's reach, keyset-paged and ordered by id. Entries omit the document and designer state.

post.description: Creates a working copy from a supplied Arazzo document, from a catalog version (the carry-over: fromBaseWorkflowId + fromVersionNumber), or blank. The deployment stamps the creator's internal tenant tag onto managementTags and rejects a set the creator's own reach does not admit.

get.description: Returns one working copy including its document and designer state. The working copy must be in the caller's reach (404 otherwise).

put.description: Saves the working copy: replaces the document (and optionally the name/designer state). The save presents the etag it read (expectedEtag) and conflicts (409) when stale — a collaborator saved first; re-fetch and reconcile. Never mints a catalog version.

delete.description: Deletes a working copy. Unconditional (a working copy is development state; the catalog is the durable record). 404 when absent or out of reach.

post.description: Runs full diagnostics over the working copy's stored Arazzo document: JSON-Schema conformance (positioned schema findings) plus document-local semantic checks mirroring the workflow compiler's rules. Always 200 with the findings (an invalid document is a successful validation run); 404 when the working copy is absent or out of reach. The document is never modified.

200.description: The validation outcome (valid flag + every positioned finding).

get.description: The scenario set travels WITH the working copy (design §4.2/§9) and is published into the package as metadata/scenarios.json.

post.summary: Run every scenario as a suite
post.description: Executes each scenario against the deterministic simulator (§8) and returns the suite report. POST on the collection is run-all; POST on a member's /run is run-one. 400 when this deployment offers no simulation.

put.description: Upserts one scenario by name (the body's name must match the path). The working copy's etag advances; the response carries the fresh etag.

200.description: The stored scenario plus the working copy's new etag.

post.description: Executes the named scenario against the deterministic simulator and returns its result: the outcome, every expectation's verdict, and the full trace (the debug tray renders it directly). 400 when this deployment offers no simulation.

post.summary: Publish the working copy as a new catalog version
post.description: The deliberate publish act (design §4.6): validates the document, re-runs the FULL scenario suite server-side (evidence is server-attested, never client-submitted), builds the package embedding metadata/scenarios.json + metadata/evidence.json, and adds the new version to the catalog. Fails 422 with the diagnostics when the document is invalid, or with the suite report when a scenario fails and requireScenarios is not disabled. The new version starts as a draft until promoted.

requireScenarios.description: Refuse to publish when any scenario fails (or when validation reports errors).

201.description: The new catalog version (a draft until promoted).

422.description: Not publishable: validation errors, or the scenario suite failed.

get.description: The schema-metadata document RECOMPUTED for the working copy's current document and attached sources (the same shape getCatalogWorkflowSchemas serves baked into a package): each workflow's typed inputs and each step's resolved operation, typed request/responses (or message), and outputs — powering the designer's typed forms (scenario inputs, mock bodies by declared status, expectations) and expression completions.

post.summary: Pull the working copy from its bound branch
post.description: Refreshes the Git-bound working copy from its branch (workflow-designer design §4.7): the document from gitBinding.path, each specPaths entry as an inline source attachment, and — when scenariosDir is bound — the scenario set from its <name>.scenario.json files. The pull is an etag-guarded save (409 when stale). Reads run on the CALLER's brokered token (409 github-not-connected without a session); 400 when the working copy has no binding or the deployment brokers no App; 404 when a bound file is missing (nothing is partially applied).

expectedEtag.description: The etag the client read (409 when stale).

ref.description: The branch, tag, or commit to pull from (default: the bound branch's head). Pulling at a commit is the git-history ROLLBACK — it replaces the working copy with that commit's state, so the UI danger-confirms it.

post.summary: Commit the working copy to its bound branch
post.description: Writes the Git-bound working copy to its branch (workflow-designer design §4.7): the document to gitBinding.path, each bound spec (specPaths) from its inline attachment, and — when scenariosDir is bound — one <name>.scenario.json per scenario. Commits run on the CALLER's brokered token and are authored as their GitHub-held git identity — the control plane composes no author/committer. Optionally opens a pull request FROM the bound branch (draft → the review flow). 400 unbound/not-brokered; 409 github-not-connected.

message.description: The commit message (each written file carries it).

pullRequest.title: GitCommitPullRequest
pullRequest.description: Open a pull request from the bound branch after the commit.

base.description: The base branch the pull request targets.

title.description: The pull request title (default: the commit message).

draft.description: Open as a draft (the review flow for workflow development).

200.description: What was written (and the pull request, when opened).

post.summary: Start a DEBUG RUN of the working copy's stored document in a development-class environment (workflow-designer design §18): a durable run under the environment's credential bindings — the runner resolves secrets as its own identity; nothing reaches the browser. Forward-only; gated by the environment's allowsDraftRuns flag, the caller's entitlement, and per-source credential readiness. Every start is audited.

201.description: The debug run, started (possibly already paused before its first step).

403.description: The environment does not allow draft runs, or the caller lacks entitlement to run there.

409.description: Credential readiness is incomplete — the problem detail lists each declared source without a credential binding in the target environment.

get.summary: The debug run's current state: status, cursor, the trace so far (the same shape simulation emits — exchanges as sent), and any wait it is suspended on.

delete.summary: Delete the debug run and purge its captured draft and metadata trace; idempotent.

204.description: The debug run was deleted (or did not exist).

post.summary: Advance the debug run: plain resume continues to the next pause point (pause.afterEachStep = single-step; pause.beforeSteps = breakpoints); an optional action applies the SAME ResumeRequest union the runs view uses — Skip with outputs is 'step over', Rewind deliberately re-executes.

200.description: The debug run after the advance.

409.description: The run is not in a resumable state.

post.summary: Cancel the debug run; a terminal state, idempotent.

Debug-only: deliver a message to a debug run that is suspended on an AsyncAPI receive, standing in for the real publisher so the run advances. The payload is delivered STRAIGHT to the awaiting run (matched by channel and, when set, correlationId) — nothing is published to a real broker, and there is no production analogue (a live workflow's message arrives from its real source). Answers the 'what if the webhook arrives now / late / malformed?' question.

200.description: The debug run after the injected message was delivered and it advanced.

409.description: The run is not suspended on a message wait for that channel.

post.description: Compiles the working copy's document (with its attached sources) and executes it against the scripted mock transport and a virtual clock, returning the structured trace up to the stop condition. Stateless interactive stepping: there is no debug-session resource — every command replays from the start (determinism makes the replay exact), so 'step' is this call with `until` one step further and time-travel scrubbing needs no further calls. Simulation never touches real credentials or endpoints; the mock transport is the only I/O surface. 200 with the trace (a faulted workflow is a successful simulation); 400 when this deployment offers no simulation or the scenario is unusable; 404 when the working copy is absent or out of reach; 422 when the document does not compile to an executable workflow.

200.description: The structured trace up to the stop condition.

422.description: The document does not compile to an executable workflow (e.g. it references another Arazzo document as a source).

get.summary: List a working copy's attached sources
get.description: Lists the working copy's attachments (no documents — the operations endpoint projects them). 404 when the working copy is absent or out of reach.

get.summary: One attachment WITH its stored document (inline) or registry reference — the full payload needed to re-attach it (restore after a detach).

200.description: The attachment, including the inline document when the working copy stores one.

document.description: The stored source document (inline attachments only).

put.summary: Attach a source to a working copy
put.description: Attaches (or replaces) the source for a sourceDescriptions name: a registry reference or an inline document — exactly one. The attachment is saved onto the working copy under optimistic concurrency; a concurrent save conflicts (409) and the client retries against the fresh state. A registry reference must name a source in the caller's reach (404 otherwise).

delete.summary: Detach a source from a working copy
delete.description: Removes the attachment for a sourceDescriptions name. 404 when the working copy or the attachment is absent.

get.summary: List an attached source's operation surface
get.description: Projects the attached source's operation surface (raw-JSON-Schema request/parameter/response shapes) for the operation browser and the step inspector's templates. A registry attachment resolves the registered source at read time (reach-checked). 404 when the working copy, the attachment, or the referenced registered source is absent.

get.summary: List a registered source's operation surface
get.description: Projects the registered source's operation surface (raw-JSON-Schema request/parameter/response shapes) — browse a source before attaching it. 404 when the source is absent or out of reach.

post.summary: Fetch a source document from a web endpoint
post.description: Fetches an OpenAPI/AsyncAPI/Arazzo document server-side (no browser CORS), optionally authenticating with a registered source credential referenced by (sourceName, environment). Returns the validated document with its detected type/version and content digest; the caller then attaches it to a working copy or registers it. 400 when the URL/scheme is not permitted, the payload is not a parseable JSON/YAML document, it declares no recognisable type, or fetching is not configured in this deployment; 404 when the referenced credential is absent or out of reach; 502 when the upstream endpoint fails.

502.description: The upstream endpoint failed (unreachable, error status, or over the size cap).

get.description: Returns a page of deployment environments the caller's reach admits (§14.2), ordered by name. Page with `limit` and the opaque `pageToken` from a previous page's `nextPageToken`.

post.description: Creates a governed, reach-scoped environment and grants the calling principal administration of it (design §7.7). The deployment stamps the creator's internal tenant tag onto managementTags and rejects a set the creator's own reach does not admit (no privilege escalation). Conflicts if an environment with that name already exists in the caller's reach.

put.description: Replaces the mutable metadata (display name, description) of an existing environment; the name, managementTags reach scope, and created-* audit fields are immutable. The caller must be a current administrator of the environment (403 otherwise); a stale concurrent change conflicts (409).

delete.description: Deletes an environment. The caller must be a current administrator of the environment (403 otherwise); a stale concurrent change conflicts (409).

get.description: Returns the administrator identities entitled to govern the environment (design §7.7) — who may manage it and approve promotions into it. Each identity is described as the deployment-mapped grant {dimension,value} it resolves from, never raw internal tags.

put.summary: Transfer environment administration (replace the administrator set)
put.description: Replaces the entire administrator set for the environment with the given identities (at least one) — an administrator may transfer administration away from itself (design §7.7). The caller must be a current administrator (403 otherwise); a stale concurrent change conflicts (409).

post.description: Adds an identity to the environment's administrator set (idempotent). The caller must be a current administrator (403 otherwise); a stale concurrent change conflicts (409).

200.description: The administrator set after the add.

delete.description: Removes the administrator identified by its identity digest (from AdministratorGrant.digest in the list) from the environment's administrator set. The set may not be left empty — removing the last administrator conflicts (409). The caller must be a current administrator (403 otherwise). An unknown digest is a no-op (the resulting set is returned).

200.description: The administrator set after the removal.

get.summary: List the workflow versions available in an environment
get.description: Returns a page of availability entries for this environment — the (workflow, version) pairs made available in it (design §7.8), ordered by base workflow id then version. Visible to a caller whose reach admits the environment.

get.summary: Count the workflow versions available in an environment
get.description: Counts the (workflow, version) pairs made available in this environment (design §7.8), bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for the list footer). Visible to a caller whose reach admits the environment (404 otherwise). When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

200.description: The bounded count of versions available in the environment.

get.summary: List the runners that serve an environment and their authorization status
get.description: Returns a page of the runner authorizations for this environment (design §5.5) — each runner that has registered for it with its Pending/Authorized/Revoked status, ordered by runnerId. The caller must be a current administrator of the environment (403 otherwise; 404 if it is not in the caller's reach). Optionally filtered by status.

post.summary: Register a runner to serve an environment (authenticated machine principal)
post.description: Registers the calling runner to serve this environment (design §5.5/§16.4). The runner authenticates as a machine principal (client-credentials, private-key-JWT, or mTLS) and presents its self-description; the control plane derives the trusted principal from the token and binds the runner's authorization to it, rather than to the self-asserted runnerId. The registration is recorded in the runner registry (with the runner's reach tags stamped from the environment's managementTags, never trusted from the runner) and the authorization enters Pending — the runner is dispatchable only once an administrator of the environment authorizes it (see authorizeRunner). Idempotent: a runner re-registering with its own principal keeps its existing authorization status. A registration presenting a principal that differs from the one already bound to this runnerId is refused (409). 404 if the environment does not exist.

200.description: The runner's authorization to serve the environment (Pending until an administrator authorizes it).

get.summary: Count the runners that serve an environment
get.description: Counts this environment's runner authorizations (design §5.5), bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for the list footer). Same filters as listEnvironmentRunnerAuthorizations: the caller must be a current administrator of the environment (403 otherwise; 404 if it is not in the caller's reach); optionally filtered by status. When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

200.description: The bounded count of the environment's runner authorizations.

post.summary: Authorize (or reinstate) a runner to serve an environment
post.description: Authorizes the runner to serve this environment (design §5.5) — it becomes dispatchable for runs targeting the environment. This is also the return path for a quarantined runner (reinstate) and a revoked one (re-authorize): it transitions Pending, Quarantined, or Revoked to Authorized. The caller must be a current administrator of the environment (403 otherwise; 404 if it is not in the caller's reach, or if no runner with that id has registered for it). Idempotent — authorizing an already-Authorized runner returns the existing record.

delete.summary: Revoke a runner's authorization to serve an environment
delete.description: Permanently removes a compromised runner (design §5.5): the runner stops being dispatched to immediately, and any lease it currently holds is expired so an authorized peer reclaims its in-flight runs at once (its own further checkpoint writes then conflict) — the fence. The record persists with status Revoked, and a revoked runner cannot return to service without a deliberate re-authorization. For a merely faulted runner that should return without ceremony, quarantine it instead. The caller must be a current administrator of the environment (403 otherwise; 404 if it is not in the caller's reach, or no such runner authorization exists). Idempotent.

post.description: Temporarily excludes a faulted runner from new dispatch (design §5.5): it stops receiving new and orphaned work while its in-flight runs drain to completion (unlike revoke, quarantine does not fence them). The record persists with status Quarantined; reinstate it by authorizing it again, with no re-registration required. Only an Authorized runner can be quarantined — quarantining a Pending or Revoked runner conflicts (409). The caller must be a current administrator of the environment (403 otherwise; 404 if it is not in the caller's reach, or no such runner authorization exists). Idempotent — quarantining an already-Quarantined runner returns the existing record.

get.description: Returns a page of registered sources the caller's reach admits (§14.2), ordered by name. Each entry is a SourceSummary — the registered document is NOT included; fetch a single source to read it. Page with `limit` and the opaque `pageToken` from a previous page's `nextPageToken`.

post.description: Registers a reach-scoped source (design §7.6): its name, type, and OpenAPI/AsyncAPI document, plus the managementTags scoping who may see and manage it. The deployment stamps the creator's internal tenant tag onto managementTags and rejects a set the creator's own reach does not admit (no privilege escalation). Conflicts if a source with that name already exists in the caller's reach.

get.description: Returns the full source, including its registered OpenAPI/AsyncAPI document, if it is in the caller's reach.

put.description: Replaces the registered document and mutable metadata (display name, description) of an existing source; the name, type, managementTags reach scope, and created-* audit fields are immutable. The source must be in the caller's reach (404 otherwise); a stale concurrent change conflicts (409).

delete.description: Deletes a source registration. The source must be in the caller's reach (404 otherwise); a stale concurrent change conflicts (409). Per-environment credentials bound to the source are managed separately and are not removed here.

get.description: Returns a page of durable schedules (#896) the caller's reach admits (§14.2). A schedule is a durable run of the built-in scheduler workflow that fires a target version on a cron cadence; this projects each such run (its cadence, target, next occurrence, and last fire) without exposing it as a raw run. Optionally filtered to one `environment`. Page with `limit` and the opaque `pageToken` from a previous page's `nextPageToken`.

parameters.name: environment
parameters.description: Restrict to schedules pinned to this deployment environment.

post.description: Creates a durable schedule (#896): a run of the built-in scheduler workflow, pinned to an environment, that fires the target version on the given cron cadence through the governed run endpoint. The `scheduleId` is the schedule's stable, globally-unique identity; re-creating the same `scheduleId` is idempotent and returns the existing schedule. Validates that the target version exists and is available in the environment (§7.8), that the caller has reach to it, and that at least one runner serving the environment advertises scheduling (`servesSchedules`); if none does, returns `409` with guidance to enable scheduling on a runner there. A scheduled start is reach- and audit-gated exactly as an operator start is. 404 if the target version does not exist or is outside the caller's reach; 409 if it is not available in the environment, not runnable, or no scheduling runner serves the environment; 422 if the target inputs fail the version's baked inputs schema.

422.description: The target inputs failed validation against the version's baked inputs schema.

get.description: Returns one schedule (its cadence, target, next occurrence, and last fire) if it is in the caller's reach.

delete.description: Cancels the schedule, stopping further occurrences. The underlying scheduler run is cancelled; runs it already started are unaffected. The schedule must be in the caller's write reach (404 otherwise).

post.description: Starts the schedule's target version immediately with the schedule's input template, bypassing the cadence, through the same governed run endpoint an occurrence uses. Does not affect the schedule's cadence or watermark. Returns 202 with the started run id. 404 if the schedule does not exist or is outside the caller's reach; 409 if the target is not available in the environment, not runnable, or no runner serves it; 422 if the inputs fail validation.

202.description: The target run was accepted and is pending execution.

422.description: The inputs failed validation against the version's baked inputs schema.

get.description: Returns the administrator identities entitled to publish further versions of the base workflow id and to manage its administration (design §15). Each identity is described as the deployment-mapped grant {dimension,value} it resolves from — never raw internal tags.

put.description: Replaces the entire administrator set for the base workflow id with the given identities (at least one) — an administrator may transfer administration away from itself (design §15). The caller must be a current administrator (403 otherwise); a stale concurrent change conflicts (409).

post.description: Adds an identity to the base workflow id's administrator set (idempotent). The caller must be a current administrator (403 otherwise); a stale concurrent change conflicts (409).

200.description: The administrator set after the add.

delete.description: Removes the administrator identified by its identity digest (from AdministratorGrant.digest in the list) from the base workflow id's administrator set. Removing a multi-tag grantee removes the whole identity in one call. The set may not be left empty — removing the last administrator conflicts (409). The caller must be a current administrator (403 otherwise). An unknown digest is a no-op (the resulting set is returned).

200.description: The administrator set after the removal.

post.description: Mints a single-use, principal-bound state and returns the GitHub App authorize URL (workflow-designer design §4.7). The response is JSON rather than a redirect because a bearer-authenticated SPA cannot carry its token on a top-level navigation: the kit opens authorizeUrl in a popup, and when GitHub redirects the popup to the callback, the opener polls the session and closes it. 400 when this deployment brokers no GitHub App.

200.description: Navigate the browser to authorizeUrl to continue at GitHub.

get.description: GitHub redirects the browser (the kit's popup) here with ?code&state. The state — single-use, short-lived, minted by beginGitHubAuth — identifies and authenticates the principal (a top-level navigation carries no bearer token), and the code exchanges server-side; the user token never reaches the browser (workflow-designer design §4.7).

parameters.name: state
parameters.description: The single-use state minted by beginGitHubAuth.

200.description: Signed in — the popup may close (the opener polls the session).

get.description: The signed-in GitHub identity and a first page of accessible repositories for the CALLING control-plane principal — connected:false when the caller has not signed in (each principal's session is private; workflow-designer design §4.7). 400 when this deployment brokers no OAuth App.

delete.description: Drops the calling principal's brokered GitHub token. Idempotent.

get.description: The repository's branches and its default branch, through the calling principal's brokered token — the Git dialog's branch picker browses these instead of asking for free-form text. Reach is whatever the signed-in user can see. 409 (github-not-connected) when the caller has no GitHub session.

post.description: Creates a branch from a base branch's head (default: the repository's default branch), through the calling principal's brokered token — the branch-per-working-copy flow (§4.7) starts here. Creating a ref makes no commit and composes no identity. 409 (github-branch-exists) when the name is taken; 409 (github-not-connected) when the caller has no GitHub session.

get.summary: Search repositories for the pickers' typeahead
get.description: Proxies a repository search through the calling principal's brokered token, for the repository pickers' typeahead. An owner-qualified query ('dotnet/run') searches that user or organisation's repositories by name prefix — reaching public repositories the session's own listing never contains; an unqualified query searches by name across what the user can see. Returns at most a pickers-sized page. 409 (github-not-connected) when the caller has no GitHub session.

parameters.name: query
parameters.description: The typed query: 'owner/name-prefix' scopes to an owner; a bare term searches by name.

200.description: The matching repositories, most relevant first.

get.description: Proxies a contents read through the calling principal's brokered token for the open/import dialogs: a directory lists its entries; a file returns its (base64) content. Reach is whatever the signed-in user can see — a repo the user cannot read is not found. 409 (github-not-connected) when the caller has no GitHub session.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The path to browse (default: the repository root).

parameters.name: ref
parameters.description: The branch, tag, or commit (default: the default branch).

200.description: The directory listing or file content.

get.description: One page of the commit history reachable through the calling principal's brokered token, newest first — the Git pane's history browser. Scope with sha (the bound branch) and path (the bound document) to see the commits that touched the working copy; each commit can then be compared side-by-side (contents at ref) or rolled back to (pull at ref). Reach is whatever the signed-in user can see. 409 (github-not-connected) when the caller has no GitHub session.

parameters.name: sha
parameters.description: The branch, tag, or commit to start from (default: the default branch).

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: Only commits touching this path (the bound document, typically).

200.description: One page of commits, newest first.

post.description: Submits a request for elevated, time-bound access to a workflow (design §16.5). Any authenticated principal may submit; the requesting subject is taken from the caller, so a grant can never target a third party. If the requester is eligible to self-elevate exactly this, the request is auto-approved (self-elevation); otherwise it is created pending an administrator's decision.

201.description: The created request (pending, or already approved when self-elevated).

get.description: Lists access requests visible to the caller, oldest first. With baseWorkflowId, returns that workflow's request queue — the caller must be an administrator of it (403 otherwise). Without baseWorkflowId, scope selects the view: 'mine' (default) the caller's own requests; 'queue' the approver inbox — every request across the workflows the caller administers. Optionally filtered by status.

parameters.name: baseWorkflowId
parameters.description: The target workflow whose request queue to list (requires administrator of it); absent lists per scope.

parameters.name: scope
parameters.description: When baseWorkflowId is absent, which requests to list: 'mine' (default) the caller's own; 'queue' the approver inbox across every workflow the caller administers. Ignored when baseWorkflowId is present.

get.description: Counts the deployment environments the caller's reach admits (§14.2), bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for list footers). When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

200.description: The bounded count of visible deployment environments.

get.description: Counts the registered sources the caller's reach admits (§14.2), bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for list footers). When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

200.description: The bounded count of visible registered sources.

get.description: Counts the source credential bindings the caller's reach admits (§14.2), bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for list footers). When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

200.description: The bounded count of visible source credential bindings.

get.description: Counts the designer working copies the caller's reach admits (§14.2), bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for list footers). When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

200.description: The bounded count of visible designer working copies.

get.description: Counts the catalog entries matching the search that the caller's read reach admits (§14.2), bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for list footers). Same filters as searchCatalog: counts matching versions, or distinct base workflows when distinctWorkflows is set. When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

200.description: The bounded count of visible matching catalog entries.

get.description: Counts the runs matching the visibility query that the caller's read reach admits (§14.2), bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for work badges and list footers). Same filters as listRuns. When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

200.description: The bounded count of visible matching runs.

get.description: Counts access requests visible to the caller, bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for work badges and list footers). Same filters as listAccessRequests: with baseWorkflowId, that workflow's request queue (administrator required, 403 otherwise); otherwise scope selects 'mine' (default) or 'queue' (the approver inbox); optionally filtered by status. When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

parameters.name: baseWorkflowId
parameters.description: The target workflow whose request queue to count (requires administrator of it); absent counts per scope.

parameters.name: scope
parameters.description: When baseWorkflowId is absent, which requests to count: 'mine' (default) the caller's own; 'queue' the approver inbox. Ignored when baseWorkflowId is present.

200.description: The bounded count of visible access requests.

get.description: Returns a single access request. The caller must be its requester or an administrator of its target workflow (403 otherwise).

post.description: Approves a pending request, writing the capped, time-boxed grant (run access only, scoped to the workflow). The caller must be an administrator of the target workflow (403 otherwise), and must not be the request's own subject — a decision is always independent, so a request that would grant the caller access is refused (403 own-request; the requester's exit is withdraw). A request that is not pending conflicts (409); a request whose scopes are not grantable is rejected (400).

post.summary: Approve an access request as durable eligibility
post.description: Approves a pending request as durable eligibility (design §16.5.3) rather than as a live grant: writes a capped eligibility assignment scoped to the workflow, after which the requester may self-elevate JIT without re-approval (each activation independently time-boxed; self-elevation is the designed exception to the independent-decision rule, because the eligibility itself was independently decided). The caller must be an administrator of the target workflow (403 otherwise), and must not be the request's own subject (403 own-request). A request that is not pending conflicts (409); a request whose scopes are not grantable is rejected (400).

Denies a pending request. The caller must be an administrator of the target workflow (403 otherwise), and must not be the request's own subject (403 own-request — the requester withdraws instead, so decidedBy always names an independent administrator). A request that is not pending conflicts (409).
Withdraws a pending request. Only the requester (the request's own subject) may withdraw it (403 otherwise); a request that is not pending conflicts (409).

Revokes an approved grant early: the entitlement is deleted (access stops at the next resolution, fail-safe) and the request is marked revoked. The caller must be an administrator of the target workflow (403 otherwise). A request that is not an approved grant conflicts (409).

post.summary: Grant a pending access request (system-credentialed, no administrator check)
post.description: Writes the capped, time-boxed entitlement a pending request's decision authorized, WITHOUT the §15-administrator check that approve applies — the system-credentialed grant path (design §16.5.1). It exists for the bootstrapped approval workflow: a human approver drives the decision inside the workflow (delivered as the injected decision message), then the workflow's §13 system credential calls this to write the grant the decision authorized. The platform ceiling is identical to approve and holds regardless of caller — at most the requested scopes intersected with run access, bound to the requester (never a third party), reach fixed to the target workflow, expiry capped at the deployment maximum — so this can never widen to an arbitrary binding (that needs security:write). Reachable only with the narrow accessRequests:grant capability, which a deployment grants solely to the approval workflow's system credential. A request that is not pending, or whose scopes are not grantable, conflicts (409).

post.summary: Grant a pending access request as standing eligibility (system-credentialed, no administrator check)
post.description: Writes the capped eligibility assignment a pending request's decision authorized (design §16.5.3), WITHOUT the §15-administrator check that approveAsEligible applies — the system-credentialed grant path (design §16.5.1). The sibling of grantAccessRequest: where that writes a one-time active grant, this writes standing eligibility, so the requester may thereafter self-elevate this access JIT without re-approval. It exists for the bootstrapped approval workflow: when a human approver's decision is 'eligible', the workflow's §13 system credential calls this. The platform ceiling is identical to approveAsEligible and holds regardless of caller — at most the requested scopes intersected with run access, bound to the requester, reach fixed to the target workflow — so this can never widen to an arbitrary binding. Reachable only with the narrow accessRequests:grant capability, which a deployment grants solely to the approval workflow's system credential. A request that is not pending, or whose scopes are not grantable, conflicts (409).

post.summary: Settle a pending access request per its decision outcome (system-credentialed, no administrator check)
post.description: Enacts the terminal state a pending request's decision authorized, WITHOUT the §15-administrator check the caller-facing approve/deny apply — the single system-credentialed enactment path (design §16.5.1). It exists for the bootstrapped approval workflow: a human approver drives the decision inside the workflow (delivered as the injected decision message), then the workflow's §13 system credential calls this once to enact whatever was decided, so every outcome flows through the same run. 'approved' writes the capped, time-boxed grant; 'eligible' writes standing eligibility (§16.5.3); 'rejected' marks the request Denied; 'withdrawn' marks it Withdrawn. The platform ceiling on a grant is identical to approve and holds regardless of caller, so this can never widen to an arbitrary binding (that needs security:write). Reachable only with the narrow accessRequests:grant capability, which a deployment grants solely to the approval workflow's system credential. A request that is not pending, or whose scopes are not grantable, conflicts (409).

200.description: The request in its settled terminal state.

post.description: Requests that a workflow version be made available in an environment (design §7.8), for a principal who cannot make it available directly because they do not administer the target environment. The requesting identity is taken from the caller. The request is created pending an environment administrator's decision; approval makes the version available (subject to the §7.7 readiness gate at approval time).

201.description: The created request (pending an environment administrator's decision).

get.description: Lists availability requests visible to the caller, oldest first. With environment, returns that environment's request queue — the caller must be an administrator of it (403 otherwise). Without environment, scope selects the view: 'mine' (default) the caller's own requests; 'queue' the approver inbox — every request across the environments the caller administers. Optionally filtered by status.

parameters.name: environment
parameters.description: The target environment whose request queue to list (requires administrator of it); absent lists per scope.

parameters.name: scope
parameters.description: When environment is absent, which requests to list: 'mine' (default) the caller's own; 'queue' the approver inbox across every environment the caller administers. Ignored when environment is present.

get.description: Counts availability requests visible to the caller, bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for work badges and list footers). Same filters as listAvailabilityRequests: with environment, that environment's request queue (administrator required, 403 otherwise); otherwise scope selects 'mine' (default) or 'queue' (the approver inbox); optionally filtered by status. When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

parameters.name: environment
parameters.description: The target environment whose request queue to count (requires administrator of it); absent counts per scope.

parameters.name: scope
parameters.description: When environment is absent, which requests to count: 'mine' (default) the caller's own; 'queue' the approver inbox. Ignored when environment is present.

200.description: The bounded count of visible availability requests.

get.description: Returns a single availability request. The caller must be its requester or an administrator of its target environment (403 otherwise).

post.description: Approves a pending request, making the workflow version available in the target environment. The caller must be an administrator of that environment (403 otherwise), and must not be the request's own requester — a decision is always independent, even for an administrator (403 own-request; the requester's exit is withdraw). A request that is not pending conflicts (409); a request whose version is not ready in the environment — a referenced source has no usable credential there (§7.7), or the environment requires evidence and the version's attested suite is not green (workflow-designer design §4.6) — is rejected (409).

Denies a pending request. The caller must be an administrator of the target environment (403 otherwise), and must not be the request's own requester (403 own-request — the requester withdraws instead, so decidedBy always names an independent administrator). A request that is not pending conflicts (409).
Withdraws the caller's own pending request. The caller must be its requester (403 otherwise). A request that is not pending conflicts (409).

get.description: Lists runner authorizations across the environments the caller administers — the approver inbox for authorizing runners (design §5.5/§15.4), ordered by (environment, runnerId). With environment, returns that environment's authorizations and the caller must administer it (403 otherwise). Without environment, returns the inbox spanning every environment the caller administers; defaults to Pending so an administrator sees the runners awaiting authorization. Optionally filtered by status.

parameters.name: status
parameters.description: Only authorizations in this state (the inbox defaults to Pending when omitted).

parameters.name: environment
parameters.description: A single environment whose authorizations to list (requires administrator of it); absent lists the inbox across every administered environment.

get.description: Counts runner authorizations visible to the caller, bounded by the server's cap — no rows are returned (for work badges and list footers). Same filters as listRunnerAuthorizations: with environment, that environment's authorizations (administrator required, 403 otherwise); otherwise the inbox across every environment the caller administers; defaults to Pending when status is omitted. When 'capped' is true the true total meets or exceeds the cap, so 'count' is the cap.

parameters.name: status
parameters.description: Only authorizations in this state (defaults to Pending when omitted).

parameters.name: environment
parameters.description: A single environment whose authorizations to count (requires administrator of it); absent counts the inbox across every administered environment.

200.description: The bounded count of visible runner authorizations.

get.description: Returns the caller's own deployment-stamped identity as {dimension,value} grants (design §16.5.4) — the identity an administrator entry or grant must name to match the caller (so a UI can offer 'add me' and warn before a transfer would lock the caller out). Empty when the deployment is unscoped.

get.summary: The grantee kinds the deployment can resolve
get.description: Returns the grantee kinds (person/team/role/workflow) the deployment can resolve to a sys: identity, and whether an external directory is configured (design §16.5.4) — so the grantee picker offers exactly the resolvable grain instead of guessing.

Searches for grantees matching a prefix, each resolved to its exact sys: identity (design §16.5.4). Source `observed` is the store-indexed typeahead of identities the control plane has already seen (access requesters, version authors, administrators, grantees); `directory` queries the external directory when one is configured (every searchable kind unless `kind` restricts); `merged` unions the two, directory-preferred (one bounded result set — no paging). An absent source defaults to `merged` when a directory is configured, `observed` otherwise. `observed` pages with `limit` and the opaque `pageToken`.

parameters.name: kind
parameters.description: Restrict to one grantee kind (absent searches every kind the deployment supports).

parameters.name: q
parameters.description: The case-sensitive value prefix to match (empty matches all).

parameters.name: source
parameters.description: Where to resolve from: the store-indexed observed-identity typeahead, the external directory, or the two merged (directory-preferred, unpaged). Absent defaults to merged when a directory is configured, observed otherwise.

502.description: The external directory could not be reached (an explicit `directory` search only; a merged search degrades to observed results).

oauth2.description: OAuth2 bearer access tokens scoped to the control plane's capability tiers. Interactive operators use the authorization-code flow (with PKCE, or device-code at the IdP); services and the CLI use client-credentials. The authorization server is chosen by the deployment — the URLs below are placeholders.

scopes.environments:read: List and read governed deployment environments (reach-scoped).
scopes.environments:write: Create, update, and delete deployment environments and manage their administrators (current-administrator membership applies).

scopes.credentials:write: Create, update, and delete source credential bindings (references and metadata only — never secret material).

scopes.environments:write: Create, update, and delete deployment environments and manage their administrators (current-administrator membership applies).

scopes.availability:write: Make a workflow version available in an environment, or withdraw it (target-environment-administrator gated; readiness-gated).
